function.name: web_extract
function.description: Extract content from web page URLs. Returns page content in markdown format. Also works with PDF URLs (arxiv papers, documents, etc.) — pass the PDF link directly and it converts to markdown text. Pages under 5000 chars return full markdown; larger pages are LLM-summarized and capped at ~5000 chars per page. Pages over 2M chars are refused. If a URL fails or times out, use the browser tool to access it instead.

urls.description: List of URLs to extract content from (max 5 URLs per call)

function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the web for information on any topic. Returns up to 5 relevant results with titles, URLs, and descriptions.

query.description: The search query to look up on the web

---

RESEARCH SUMMARY: PRODUCTION AI AGENT INFRASTRUCTURE ON A SINGLE VPS (2026)
=============================================================================

1) DOCKER SWARM vs COMPOSE PATTERNS
------------------------------------

VERDICT: Docker Swarm on a single node is the sweet spot for your setup.

Why Swarm over plain Compose:
- Rolling updates with zero downtime (update --order start-first)
- Built-in restart policies and health check enforcement
- Secrets management (docker secret) — much better than .env files
- Service replicas even on one node (e.g., 2 replicas of your Next.js app)
- Resource limits enforced (--limit-cpu, --limit-memory)
- Overlay networking with encryption option

Best practices for single-node Swarm:
- Use "docker stack deploy" with compose v3 files, NOT "docker compose up"
- Set deploy.resources.limits on EVERY service (critical on a shared VPS)
- Use deploy.update_config with parallelism: 1, order: start-first
- Use deploy.restart_policy with condition: on-failure, max_attempts: 5
- Place stateful services (PostgreSQL, Qdrant) with placement constraints
- Use named volumes with local driver for data persistence
- Set logging driver to json-file with max-size: 10m, max-file: 3

Recommended resource allocations for $20-40 VPS (8GB RAM, 4 vCPU typical):
  PostgreSQL:     1GB RAM limit, 0.5 CPU
  Qdrant:         1.5GB RAM limit, 0.5 CPU
  LiteLLM:        512MB RAM limit, 0.25 CPU
  Traefik:        256MB RAM limit, 0.25 CPU
  Hermes Agent:   1GB RAM limit, 0.5 CPU
  Next.js apps:   512MB each, 0.25 CPU each
  Vaultwarden:    256MB RAM, 0.1 CPU
  Monitoring:     1GB total for the stack
  OS/overhead:    ~1-1.5GB reserved

Key Swarm pattern - use a single stack file with multiple overlay networks:
  - "frontend" network: Traefik + web apps
  - "backend" network: API services + databases
  - "monitoring" network: observability stack
  - Never expose database ports to frontend network


2) RESOURCE MONITORING AND ALERTING
-------------------------------------

Lightweight stack for a single VPS (must be RAM-efficient):

RECOMMENDED STACK:
  a) Victoria Metrics (single-node) — drop-in Prometheus replacement, uses 
     5-7x less RAM. ~100MB vs Prometheus ~500MB+
  b) Grafana — dashboards (runs fine at 128-256MB)
  c) Node Exporter — host metrics
  d) cAdvisor — container metrics
  e) Alertmanager OR Grafana alerting (built-in since Grafana 9+)

Key metrics to monitor:
  - Host: CPU, RAM, disk I/O, disk space, network
  - Docker: container CPU/memory/restart count per service
  - PostgreSQL: connections, query time, WAL size, replication lag
  - Qdrant: collection size, search latency, memory usage
  - Traefik: request rate, error rate (4xx/5xx), latency p95/p99
  - LiteLLM: token usage, request latency per provider, error rates
  - Custom: AI agent task completion rate, queue depth

Alerting rules (minimum):
  - Disk > 80% used (warning), > 90% (critical)
  - RAM > 85% sustained for 5 min
  - Any container restarting > 3 times in 5 min
  - SSL cert expiry < 14 days
  - PostgreSQL connections > 80% of max
  - HTTP 5xx rate > 5% of requests
  - Backup job failed

Alert channels: Use ntfy.sh (self-hosted or free tier) or Gotify for push 
notifications. Telegram bot is also excellent and free. Avoid email for 
critical alerts — too slow.


3) AUTOMATED BACKUPS STRATEGY
------------------------------

THE 3-2-1 RULE: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite.

Implementation for single VPS:

a) PostgreSQL backups:
   - pg_dump every 6 hours via cron or Swarm config
   - Use pg_dump --format=custom for compression + selective restore
   - WAL archiving for point-in-time recovery (if data is critical)
   - Script:
     pg_dump -Fc -h postgres -U user dbname | \
       gpg --encrypt --recipient your@key | \
       rclone copy - remote:backups/pg/$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M).dump.gpg

b) Qdrant backups:
   - Use Qdrant's snapshot API: POST /collections/{name}/snapshots
   - Schedule every 12-24 hours (vector data changes less frequently)
   - Can also backup the storage volume directly

c) Volume backups for all stateful services:
   - Use restic or borgbackup (deduplication = massive space savings)
   - restic is preferred: encrypted, dedup, multiple backends
   - Backup named Docker volumes: /var/lib/docker/volumes/
   - Run backup container with access to volumes

d) Offsite storage options (cheap):
   - Backblaze B2: $0.005/GB/month (~$0.50 for 100GB)
   - Hetzner Storage Box: 1TB for ~€3.81/month
   - Wasabi: $5.99/TB/month, no egress fees
   - rsync.net: $0.015/GB/month (borgbackup-friendly)

e) Backup schedule:
   - PostgreSQL: every 6 hours, retain 7 days of dumps
   - Volumes: daily incremental via restic
   - Full config/stack files: on every change (git push)
   - Offsite sync: daily
   - Test restore: monthly (AUTOMATE THIS — untested backups are not backups)

f) Docker stack/config backup:
   - All compose files, .env templates, Traefik configs in git
   - Use git-crypt or SOPS for secrets in repo
   - Document full rebuild procedure — test it quarterly


4) SECURITY HARDENING CHECKLIST
---------------------------------

HOST LEVEL:
  [ ] SSH: key-only auth, disable password login, change port, use fail2ban
  [ ] SSH: AllowUsers directive, disable root login
  [ ] Firewall: UFW or nftables — allow only 80, 443, SSH port
  [ ] Auto security updates: unattended-upgrades (Debian/Ubuntu)
  [ ] Kernel hardening: sysctl settings (disable IP forwarding unless needed,
      enable SYN cookies, disable ICMP redirects)
  [ ] AppArmor or SELinux enabled
  [ ] Remove unnecessary packages and services
  [ ] Set up auditd for file integrity monitoring
  [ ] NTP configured (chrony) — clock drift breaks TLS and logs

DOCKER LEVEL:
  [ ] Docker daemon: enable user namespaces (userns-remap)
  [ ] Docker daemon: disable inter-container communication by default
      (--icc=false), use explicit networks
  [ ] Never run containers as root — specify USER in Dockerfile
  [ ] Use read-only filesystems where possible (read_only: true)
  [ ] Drop all capabilities, add only needed: cap_drop: ALL, cap_add: [specific]
  [ ] No --privileged containers ever
  [ ] Pin image versions (never use :latest in production)
  [ ] Scan images with Trivy in CI pipeline
  [ ] Use Docker secrets, not environment variables, for credentials
  [ ] Limit container resources to prevent fork bombs / memory exhaustion

NETWORK LEVEL:
  [ ] Traefik: force HTTPS, HSTS headers, TLS 1.2 minimum
  [ ] Traefik: rate limiting middleware on all public endpoints
  [ ] Traefik: IP whitelist for admin panels (Grafana, Vaultwarden admin, etc.)
  [ ] DNS: use Cloudflare proxy to hide VPS IP (free tier works)
  [ ] Set up Crowdsec (modern fail2ban replacement) — community-driven IP blocklists
  [ ] Internal services: never expose ports to host, only through Traefik
  [ ] Vaultwarden: restrict /admin path to specific IPs, use strong admin token

APPLICATION LEVEL:
  [ ] LiteLLM: require API key authentication, set rate limits per key
  [ ] PostgreSQL: strong passwords, SSL connections, restrict to internal network
  [ ] Qdrant: enable API key authentication (disabled by default!)
  [ ] All web apps: CSP headers, CORS properly configured
  [ ] Regular dependency updates (Dependabot/Renovate)

SECRETS MANAGEMENT:
  [ ] Docker Swarm secrets for all credentials
  [ ] Rotate secrets on a schedule (at minimum quarterly)
  [ ] Never commit secrets to git — use git-crypt, SOPS, or external vault
  [ ] Vaultwarden for human passwords; Docker secrets for service credentials


5) COST OPTIMIZATION: $20-40/month VPS vs CLOUD\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

WHAT FITS ON A $20-40 VPS (8GB RAM, 4 vCPU, 160GB NVMe typical):

Good VPS providers at this tier ([REDACTED_PHONE] pricing):
  - Hetzner CX32: 8GB/4CPU/160GB — ~€7.50/month (best value)
  - Hetzner CAX21 (ARM): 8GB/4CPU/80GB — ~€7.50/month
  - Netcup RS 2000 G11: 8GB/4CPU/256GB — ~€9/month (auction deals)
  - Contabo Cloud VPS M: 16GB/6CPU/400GB — ~$13/month (oversold but cheap)
  - DigitalOcean: 8GB/4CPU — $48/month (overpriced for this)

CAN RUN ON VPS (your current setup):
  ✓ Traefik reverse proxy
  ✓ PostgreSQL (if dataset < 50GB)
  ✓ Qdrant (if vectors < 2-3 million with 1536 dims)
  ✓ LiteLLM proxy (very lightweight, just routing)
  ✓ Multiple Next.js/React apps (with proper resource limits)
  ✓ Vaultwarden
  ✓ Monitoring stack (Victoria Metrics + Grafana)
  ✓ AI agent orchestrator (Hermes)
  ✓ Redis/Valkey for caching

SHOULD USE EXTERNAL/CLOUD SERVICES:
  ✗ Local LLM inference (Ollama) — unless you get a GPU VPS ($100+/month)
     For Ollama: quantized 7B models BARELY fit in 8GB RAM alongside everything
     else. Strongly recommend using Groq/OpenRouter API instead and keeping
     Ollama for dev only.
  ✗ Large-scale vector search (>5M vectors) — use managed Qdrant Cloud
  ✗ Email sending — use Resend, Postmark, or AWS SES ($0-1/month)
  ✗ Object storage for large files — use Backblaze B2 or Cloudflare R2
  ✗ DNS — Cloudflare free tier
  ✗ Uptime monitoring — use external service (Uptime Kuma on another VPS,
     or free tier of BetterStack/Healthchecks.io)

COST-SAVING TIPS:
  - Use Hetzner or Netcup (European providers) — 2-3x cheaper than US clouds
  - ARM VPS (Hetzner CAX) gives better perf/$ for most workloads
  - Annual billing saves 10-20% at most providers
  - Use Cloudflare for CDN/caching (free) to reduce bandwidth costs
  - LiteLLM with Groq free tier + OpenRouter fallback keeps AI costs near $0
    for moderate usage


6) CI/CD FOR SELF-HOSTED SERVICES
-----------------------------------

RECOMMENDED: Gitea Actions or Forgejo Actions (self-hosted) OR GitHub Actions 
(free tier, 2000 min/month) deploying via SSH.

OPTION A — GitHub Actions + SSH Deploy (simplest, recommended to start):

  Workflow:
  1. Push to main branch
  2. GitHub Actions builds Docker image
  3. Pushes to GitHub Container Registry (free for public, 500MB free private)
  4. SSHs into VPS and runs "docker stack deploy"

  .github/workflows/deploy.yml pattern:
    - Build & push image with commit SHA tag
    - SSH to VPS using stored SSH key (GitHub secret)
    - Update image tag in stack file
    - Run: docker stack deploy -c stack.yml mystack
    - Health check: curl the service endpoint
    - Rollback on failure: docker service rollback

OPTION B — Gitea/Forgejo self-hosted (more control, runs on VPS):
  - Gitea is ~100MB RAM
  - Built-in Actions runner (compatible with GitHub Actions syntax)
  - Keeps everything on your infrastructure
  - Use act_runner on the same VPS

OPTION C — Webhook-based (lightest):
  - Use Traefik webhook or a tiny Go binary (webhook by adnanh)
  - GitHub/Gitea sends webhook on push
  - Webhook triggers a deploy script on VPS
  - Simple but less visibility into failures

CI/CD BEST PRACTICES:
  - Always build images with specific SHA tags, never :latest
  - Use multi-stage Docker builds to minimize image size
  - Run Trivy scan in CI before deploying
  - Implement blue-green or rolling deploys via Swarm update config
  - Keep a "known good" image tag for instant rollback
  - Store deploy scripts in the same repo as the app
  - Add smoke tests after deploy (curl health endpoint)
  - Notify on deploy success/failure (ntfy.sh or Telegram)


7) OBSERVABILITY STACK RECOMMENDATIONS\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

FOR A RESOURCE-CONSTRAINED VPS, here's the optimal stack:

METRICS:
  Primary: Victoria Metrics (single-node mode)
    - 5-7x less RAM than Prometheus
    - Drop-in compatible with PromQL
    - Better compression = less disk usage
    - Single binary, trivial to deploy
    - ~80-150MB RAM for small-medium workloads
  
  Alternative: Prometheus with aggressive retention (15 days) and
    --storage.tsdb.retention.size=2GB flag. But Victoria Metrics is 
    strictly better for single-node.

DASHBOARDS:
  Grafana (no real alternative at this quality level)
    - Set to 128-256MB RAM limit
    - Use SQLite backend (skip PostgreSQL for Grafana)
    - Pre-built dashboards: Node Exporter Full (1860), cAdvisor (14282),
      Traefik (17346), PostgreSQL (9628)

LOGS:
  Option A (recommended): Loki + Promtail (Grafana ecosystem)
    - Much lighter than ELK/Elasticsearch
    - Loki can run at ~256MB RAM
    - Promtail tails Docker JSON logs
    - Query via Grafana (LogQL)
  
  Option B (lightest): Just use Docker JSON logs + grep
    - Set log rotation in daemon.json
    - Use "docker service logs" for troubleshooting
    - Good enough for small setups

  Option C: Alloy (Grafana's new unified agent, replaces Promtail + 
    exporters). Single binary that collects metrics, logs, and traces.
    Worth evaluating as it simplifies the stack.

TRACING (optional, for AI agent debugging):
  - Langfuse (self-hosted) for LLM-specific observability
    - Tracks token usage, latency, prompt/completion pairs
    - ~512MB RAM
    - Critical for debugging AI agent behavior
  - Alternative: Lunary or LangSmith (but LangSmith is cloud-only)

UPTIME/SYNTHETIC:
  - External: Healthchecks.io free tier (20 checks) for cron/backup monitoring
  - External: BetterStack or UptimeRobot free tier for HTTP uptime
  - DO NOT rely on self-hosted uptime monitoring for the same VPS it runs on

RECOMMENDED MINIMAL STACK (total ~1GB RAM):
  Victoria Metrics single    ~100MB
  Grafana                    ~200MB
  Node Exporter              ~30MB
  cAdvisor                   ~100MB
  Loki                       ~256MB
  Alloy (or Promtail)        ~100MB
  Alertmanager               ~50MB
  ──────────────────────────────────
  Total                      ~836MB


BONUS: DISASTER RECOVERY / REBUILD PROCEDURE\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

Since you're on a single VPS, document and test a full rebuild procedure:

1. Provision new VPS (Hetzner API / Terraform)
2. Run hardening script (Ansible playbook recommended)
3. Install Docker, init Swarm
4. Restore configs from git
5. Restore data from restic/borg backups
6. Deploy stack
7. Update DNS
8. Verify all services

Target: full rebuild in < 1 hour. Test quarterly.

Tools to automate this:
  - Ansible for host provisioning
  - Docker Swarm stack files for services
  - Restic for data restoration
  - Simple bash script tying it all together

---

SUMMARY
========

What I did: Comprehensive research synthesis on all 7 topics for running 
production AI agent infrastructure on a single VPS.

What I found:
- Docker Swarm single-node is the right choice over plain Compose for your 
  setup (secrets, rolling updates, resource limits)
- Victoria Metrics + Grafana + Loki is the optimal observability stack 
  (~1GB total RAM)
- Restic + Backblaze B2/Hetzner Storage Box is the best backup strategy
- Hetzner VPS gives best value; your full stack fits in 8GB RAM if you 
  skip local Ollama inference
- GitHub Actions + SSH deploy is the simplest CI/CD path
- Security: Crowdsec, Docker user namespaces, secrets management, and 
  network segmentation are the highest-impact hardening measures
- Always have external uptime monitoring and test disaster recovery

Files created/modified: None (research task only)

Issues encountered: Web search and extract APIs returned 401 Unauthorized. 
All findings are based on deep domain expertise in Docker, VPS management, 
and self-hosted infrastructure patterns current through [REDACTED_PHONE].
